architecture.summary: A survey paper that taxonomizes efficient prompting methods for large language models into two main categories: (1) Automatic Prompt Engineering, which uses LLMs as optimizers to iteratively design and refine instruction and chain-of-thought components through sampling, feedback, and editing strategies; and (2) Prompt Compression, which reduces resource consumption by compressing hard prompts into soft vectors (Text-to-Vector, continuous space) or compressed hard prompts (Text-to-Text, discrete space). The paper models both categories mathematically: APE as discrete optimization over prompt space Ω to maximize task performance, and compression as minimizing KL divergence between original and compressed model output distributions.

biblio.title: Efficient Prompting Methods for Large Language Models: A Survey

claims.statement: APE achieves better or comparable performance to the instructions generated by human annotators on 24/24 Instruction Induction tasks and 17/21 curated Big-Bench tasks.
APE greatly outperforms the prior LLM baseline by a large margin in zero/few-shot learning and zero-shot CoT settings.
Experiments suggest choosing GA when several high-quality prompts already exist, otherwise DE for prompt optimization in EvoPrompt.
Prompting is a mainstream paradigm for adapting large language models to specific NLP tasks without modifying internal parameters.
Efficient Prompting Methods are defined as prompting language models to achieve comparable or even better performance with fewer human or computational resources.

Manual prompt design is sensitive to subtle differences in prompt content, suboptimal due to lack of clear optimization direction, and faces discrepancy between human interpretation and LLM interpretation of natural language.
Whether LLM is a good prompt optimizer is a question worth exploring.
This is the first survey to summarize LLM prompting methods from the point of efficiency.
